description: A bus-mounted camera system that issues violations to vehicles occupying bus lanes, to double parked vehicles along bus routes, and to vehicles blocking bus stops. Administered in partnership between the MTA, NYC Department of Finance (DOF), and NYC Department of Transportation (DOT). The 2023 NYS Legislature passed a provision in the budget to expand ACE. Currently active on 60 bus routes.

ACE will be implemented in May 2024 as a four-year demonstration. Activated on fourteen routes in May 2024, expand to another 400 buses and fourteen additional routes, then add ten routes per year from 2025 through 2027
Expansion of ACE program beyond bus lanes to vehicles blocking bus stops and double-parked vehicles. Cameras added to up to 1,000 buses and enforced on 28 bus routes.
Expanded scope of ABLE program to also monitor and ticket vehicles obstructing bus stops and double-parked vehicles, following legislative approval.
Expanded scope of ABLE program to include monitoring and ticketing vehicles obstructing bus stops as well as double-parked vehicles, following legislative approval

Expansion of the Automated Bus Lane Enforcement Program (ABLE), renamed ACE. Issues tickets to cars illegally parked in bus stops and bus lanes. Launched June 2024 on 14 existing ABLE routes with state-of-the-art enforcement technology. NYS Budget approved expansion to enforce bus lanes, bus stops, and illegal double parking during a four-year demonstration period.

Activated ACE on 34 routes in partnership with NYC DOT and NYC DOF. As of November 29, all routes transitioned from warning period to issuing violations. Bus lane speeds increased on average by 5%, 20% reduction in collisions, 5-10% estimated reduction in emissions. Covers 1,000 buses.

Expansion of Automated Camera Enforcement (ACE) adding 10 new bus routes on September 16, 2024 and another 10 on September 30, 2024. Total reaching 34 routes and over 1,000 ACE-equipped buses across all five boroughs.
Program issuing tickets for double parking, blocking bus stops, and driving in bus lanes via bus-mounted cameras
expand ACE to 30 additional routes and 1,000 more buses, beginning with the M2 and M4 at the end of April
Covers 39 routes with over 1,200 equipped buses serving 775,000 daily riders; includes bus lane, bus stop, and double-parking enforcement

Modification 2 to Hayden for bus stop and double-parked vehicle enforcement and up to 1,000 additional ACE systems; Modification 1 to Seon for bus stop, double-parked, bike lane, and paratransit stop enforcement on up to 1,000 ACE systems

Started as Automated Bus Lane Enforcement (ABLE) in 2019, evolved into ACE. Uses cameras on buses to enforce bus lanes, bus stops, and double-parking rules. Active on 58 routes, covering about 560 miles of streets across all five boroughs.
Automated Camera Enforcement system for bus lanes, bus stops, double-parked vehicles, bike lanes, and paratransit stops
status: 60-day warning period started September 16, 2024 on 10 new bus routes; additional 10 routes starting September 30, 2024

description: Finalized early 2025. Includes simplifying, straightening, and consolidating routes, $30 million of new/increased service, new 'rush' limited routes. Set to go into effect June 2025. Did not include new SBS routes.
Reimagined bus network for Queens, driven by customer feedback, implementing Rush routes and modernized service across the borough

The Queens Bus Network Redesign aims to create a more efficient, reliable, and accessible bus system that better serves the needs of all riders, while prioritizing equity to address disparities in access and opportunity.

Proposed Final Plan for redesigning the Queens bus network, published Q4 2023. Proposes 121 total routes (91 local, 30 express) vs 113 existing routes. Includes bus priority expansion with NYC DOT, overnight network expansion, all-day frequent network expansion, and bus stop spacing changes.
Queens Bus Network Redesign Proposed Final Plan Addendum published December 2024
Board staff summary seeking approval to implement the Queens Bus Network Redesign as set forth in the December 2024 Proposed Final Plan Addendum for local and express bus service in Queens.

MTA's borough-wide plan to improve bus service in Queens, launched in 2019. Coordinating with NYC DOT on targeted corridors, including Grand Av/Grand St. Published Proposed Final Plan in December 2023.
Queens Bus Network Redesign New Draft Plan released in March 2022, one of the largest bus network redesigns in the country with over 100 routes
Targeting to finalize Queens Bus Network Redesign next year
Seeks to modernize the borough's bus network through a comprehensive redesign to deliver faster, more frequent, and more reliable service. Proposed Final Plan announced in December 2023.

Proposal to redesign the Queens bus network to better match ridership demands while improving service for customers. Simpler, more direct routes, improved bus stop balancing, enhanced connectivity, improved frequency on key routes, and expanded bus priority.
Borough-by-borough bus network redesign process; Proposed Final Plan for more robust bus service released in December 2023; public consultation process in March 2024
The Committee authorized a public hearing on the Queens Bus Network Redesign
Queens Bus network redesign focused on simplifying and improving service for customers with more reliable service, faster travel, better connections, and improved ease of use
Queens Bus network redesign focused on simplifying and improving service for customers with more reliable service, faster travel, better connections, and improved ease of use.

Support for the Queens Bus Network Redesign, including funding for an additional 47 bus operators and 10 managers at NYCT and an additional 78 positions at MTA Bus Company (NYC Funded). Funded as a New Need/Investment in the November Financial Plan.
Queens network redesign mentioned as a priority initiative for expense growth
Queens Bus Network Redesign New Needs/Investments: $13 million in 2025, $25 million in 2026, $26 million in 2027 and 2028
Proposed redesign of Queens bus network aiming to improve route design, connectivity, frequency, and accessibility. Will be implemented in two phases, scheduled for summer and fall of 2025.
Public hearing held in Queens to discuss the Queens Bus Network Redesign; feedback provided by customers will help inform next steps.
Increase Appeal for Customers - Provide 21st Century Bus Service
Proposed Final Plan for the Queens Bus Network Redesign

Board approval to implement the Queens Bus Network Redesign described in the December 2024 Proposed Final Plan Addendum for local and express bus service in Queens. Includes 49 existing routes with routing changes, 17 new routes, and four retired routes.
The Board approved the Queens Bus Network Redesign on February 26, 2025. Chair Lieber stated it would be funded by savings identified through the efficiency effort.
Redesign of Queens bus network, rolling out in two phases at end of June and end of August
Bus network redesign for Queens with two-phase implementation

Transformative initiative launching in Phase 1 on June 29, 2025. 16 new routes will launch, 67 routes will be modified, 5 discontinued in Phase One. In Phase Two, 1 new route, 37 modified, 1 discontinued.
Historic effort to bring new and improved transit service to Queens, introducing rush routes and enhanced coverage. Phase 1 takes effect June 29, 2025.

Historic effort to bring new and improved transit service to Queens. Includes introduction of rush routes, enhanced coverage in Queens neighborhoods, and a promotional free transfer policy allowing Queens bus riders tapping with OMNY one additional free bus transfer within a three-hour period.
Launched Phase 1 in Eastern Queens; Phase 2 in Northwest and Southwest Queens starting August 31
Temporary impact of hiring for Queens Bus Network Redesign in advance of (QBNR) beginning
Queens Bus Network Redesign including route changes for Q102, B62, Q103 starting August 21

One of the largest and most complex bus network redesigns in North American history, implemented in two phases during summer 2025. Introduced a modernized bus network for Queens. Six-year effort with three rounds of proposals, more than 18,000 comments, over 250 events.
Full rollout of a new bus network for Queens, launched in two phases over summer 2025, added 11 new routes, expanded overnight service, improved transit access for 68,000 more residents
Queens Bus Network Redesign service improvements highlighted as recent accomplishment
Contributed to MTA Bus non-reimbursable overtime unfavorable variance of $1M YTD September 2025
Releasing a New Draft Plan for the Queens Bus Network Redesign in March 2022, based on over 11,000 comments received; virtual public workshops to be held spring and summer 2022

A reimagined plan, driven by customer feedback, to redesign the bus network in Queens with simpler routes, better connections, improved frequency, better bus stop spacing, and more busways and bus lanes
One of the largest bus network redesigns in the country, with over 100 routes, serving almost 800K average weekday riders. Draft plan released March 29, 2022
Queens Bus Network Redesign Project overview presented at the May 25, 2022 Board meeting by NYCT President Richard Davey and Dorian Statom, Director, Bus Service Planning
The Queens Bus Network Redesign team hosted 40 events over the past five months to gather feedback on the new draft plan. Final plan expected to be published in 2023.
The Queens Bus Network Redesign Team hosted 39 events to gather feedback, including 19 virtual events and 20 in-person.

Capital project under MTA Capital Construction & Development

Part of the MTA Expansion Program with $58.3 million added to the 2020-2024 Capital Program. Element # G80801, new project with $58,261,183 budget. Funded by $43 million from the State of New York (increasing envelope) and $15 million from the federal RAISE grant program.

Full design beginning, released RFP for preliminary engineering team to advance the project. With 2025-2029 capital plan funding, will be able to complete design for NYC's first light rail line serving more than 100,000 daily riders, connecting neighborhoods in Brooklyn and Queens.
Full design beginning; RFP released for preliminary engineering team. NYC's first light rail line, will serve more than 100,000 daily riders and connect neighborhoods in Brooklyn and Queens.
NYC's first light rail line which will serve more than 100,000 daily riders and connect neighborhoods in Brooklyn and Queens
NYC's first light rail line serving more than 100,000 daily riders connecting neighborhoods in Brooklyn and Queens

Transformative light rail train project in existing rail corridor between Bay Ridge and Jackson Heights. C&D is pursuing FTA funding, engaging public outreach including 35+ meetings. Design progressing with survey and design work prioritizing minimizing bridge reconstruction, improving subway connections, reducing station costs. Next steps include continuing outreach and preparing RFP for preliminary engineering.
The proposed 2025-2029 Capital Plan includes funding for the Interborough Express (IBX).
MTA C&D project listed in Federal FFY 2025 grant request
NYS Budget to Fund Capital Plan - Interborough Express route in Brooklyn from Roosevelt Avenue to Brooklyn Army Terminal

Transformative new rapid transit service between Brooklyn and Queens. Transforming a lightly used freight railway into a light-rail line serving almost 1 million nearby residents and connecting 17 subway lines, more than 50 bus routes and Long Island Rail Road.
MTA Interborough Express - Advancing the IBX
Light rail service connecting Brooklyn and Queens, delivered in two phases. Phase 1 prepares the corridor; Phase 2 installs the light rail system.
Light rail transit connecting Brooklyn and Queens from Sunset Park to Jackson Heights, 19 stations, 14-mile corridor, approximately 32 minute end-to-end runtime, projected daily ridership of 160,000.
Environmental review underway, running concurrently with the design contract awarded in August. First public meeting of the environmental review will take place at Brooklyn College.
$58.3 million to support design for the Interborough Express project (IBX)
Formal kickoff of design and engineering for the Interborough Express project
Formal kickoff of design and engineering for the Interborough Express project, with anticipated collaboration with the City.
Large scale expansion project referenced during public hearing for FFY 2026. Public hearing comments noted significant support for the Interborough Express (IBX).
Transit link serving historically underserved communities in Brooklyn and Queens, connecting them to subway, bus, and LIRR, serving 900,000 residents
Light rail transit system along an existing rail corridor from Sunset Park to Jackson Heights, approximately 14 miles with 18 stations, ~32 minute end-to-end runtime, 160,000 projected daily riders.
Next Phase of IBX Outreach

New transit connection linking Brooklyn and Queens along existing 14-mile freight rail corridor between Sunset Park and Jackson Heights. 18 stations, connections to 17 subway lines, 50+ bus routes, and LIRR. Light rail selected as preferred mode in 2023. Design RFP issued 2024. Included in MTA Capital Plan 2025, not yet fully funded. Environmental review/DEIS anticipated later this year (2026).
Next phase of IBX outreach advancing quickly with another six community workshops. Full details of events on MTA website.
Proposed 14-mile light rail line connecting Brooklyn waterfront at Bay Ridge with Jackson Heights Queens
status: full design beginning, RFP released for preliminary engineering team
full design to begin; RFP released for preliminary engineering team
proposed for federal funding in FFY 2025
preliminary design contract award pending Board approval
design development and environmental review underway

description: Currently in planning stages. Draft plan contains proposal for new SBS route between Kensington, Brooklyn and JFK Airport (B55 SBS). Final plan expected in second half of 2025.
Draft Plan for the Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign, proposing a reimagined Brooklyn bus network with routes, stops, and generalized frequencies.

Comprehensive redesign of the Brooklyn bus network with proposed routes, stops, and generalized frequencies reimagined to meet the needs of current and future customers. The Draft Plan was published December 1, 2022, proposing 69 local bus routes (54 Local, 3 Rush, 7 Limited, 5 Crosstown/SBS) plus express routes.

Release of the Proposed Final Plan expected in late 2025. Addresses concerns of riders and other stakeholders received in Draft Plan feedback. Public will have another opportunity to give feedback through additional round of outreach.

The MTA released a draft plan for the Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign – the first overhaul of Brooklyn bus service in decades. The proposals create a simpler bus network to encourage bus ridership, reduce travel time and strengthen interborough bus travel. The plan proposes 69 local routes and 19 express routes, for a total of 88 routes.
Aims to create a simpler bus network to encourage bus ridership, reduce travel time and strengthen interborough bus travel
Draft Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign Plan released last month (December 2022), the first overhaul of Brooklyn bus service in decades
A draft Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign includes changes to local and express buses will be released later this year
Outreach pop-up events and participation at NYTM BusFest 2023
Draft Plan published; 15 in-person public events held between April 14 and May 11, 2023; 18 virtual workshops held January-March 2023
OP is preparing the Proposed Final Plan based on the nearly 8,000 comments received in late 2022 and 2023

Part of NYCT's larger effort to modernize New York City's bus network and improve bus service borough by borough. The Draft Plan proposed 69 local routes and 19 express routes to better serve the borough's 650,000 weekday riders.
status: Draft Plan released in late 2022; Proposed Final Plan expected later this year (2024)
